speaker: interviewer
text: It's November 17, 1995. Magda, we left off talking about the conditions in Cyprus.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else that stands out in your mind that happened on Cyprus?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'll tell you. What happened on Cyprus, you know, I mentioned that the entertainment was, sometimes, boxing. And then when you have big crowds, you know, there was a boxing match. I was-- I was pregnant at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] some people protested that the winner should be the other side, you know. And then they were starting, I'd say, a revolution. And they were coming from back, you know, jumping. I was lucky on that-- on that I could have been trampled to death, you know? And in the back they were knifing each other. See, in Cyprus, some people were very much on the edge. And there were all different nationalities. There were people from Morocco, you know, and everywhere. And some of them were not even-- you know-- different-- different personalities, too. And when you have many people locked in-- in tents for a year, you know, sometimes it's-- you have revolutions, you know what I mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: But mainly, we kept occupied. Because I was my husband's secretary and his assistant. And I worked with him for a year. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was-- in that sense, it was very educational for me, you know. And I felt I'd do something-- something useful. But I always like to take a positive attitude in life, you know. In a very negative situation, I always try to. Because this is the secret of survival. You have to be positive in negative situation or extreme adverse situation. Because, otherwise, you'll never make it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was-- I was happy. We were very happy to leave Cyprus. But we had some real hard years in Israel, you know. Because it was right after the war. We didn't even know if the armistice is going to be maintained.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and you know, we-- we had the first insight into the whole Palestinian question. I can tell you exactly, you know, many things-- how it started and maybe how it could have been prevented. You see, when Israel was occupied-- when-- when Israel occupied, won the war-- then people like, you know, many people from Haifa, the-- the Arabs, were running away. Because they were told that Israel is going to go and kill them, you know. And all Israel was in-- in-- it could hardly maintain and give lodging and food for the people who came, you know. And they did not say that they should go away, not what they wanted. Nobody would have done anything to them. And then, these-- all these Arab states, which were very, very rich in oil-- they didn't take care of their people. And then it started, you know. You know, everything starts-- a fire starts from a little spark. And I think this whole thing started from a little spark, which then-- it-- it developed into great flames.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you come to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: We came to America in 1957. And our first place was New York. We have a relative, my mother's cousin, who lived in New York at that time. Now, he's still living, in West Palm Beach. And then we were just a short period in New York, maybe several months. And then my husband got a position at the Yale University. And he was teaching at the University for a year. And then there was an-- an opening for the Augusta, Georgia medical college. And there was a neurosurgeon who was looking for somebody who can be a partner. So we moved there. And-- and then there was an opening for a neurosurgeon in Monroe, Wisconsin. And my husband established the first neurosurgical department there, because my husband is like pioneer work, you know. He likes challenge. And we were for 16 years then. He was associated with the Monroe Clinic and St. Clare Hospital. And then we-- after 16 years, we came to Dubuque and Iowa. And my husband established there another neurosurgical department. And the first one, because they need a neurosurgeon in [INAUDIBLE] Hospital, in order there should be a trauma center. And-- and so he was working very hard. He was for 45 years in neurosurgery. And now, as he retired, the story is that we moved away. I left again. I always feel, sometimes, uprooted from here to there. So I was uprooted from my friends. And-- but then, you know, there is always a new beginning. And I like to be in Arizona. And it's beautiful. The winters are beautiful. And then we like to hike. And my husband like, loves the outdoors. And he pulls me out. [INAUDIBLE] And-- and then we like the month, so in summer, when this is very hot here, my daughter stays here all year long. So he is very happy when he can-- she can escape. She was ten days with us in Colorado. And then I have a son. And I have grandchildren. And this is-- this is where I am at now.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are your present interests?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my present interest is number one-- is what your number one interest is, the Holocaust. I would still love to-- to have my public speaking, which I did for 22 years, in schools, in organizations, in churches, in-- in temples, you know. It-- I introduced the Holocaust everywhere where I was. And in some strange ways, everywhere where where I was, I was the only survivor. So I am happy that I could I could-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] teach people what the Holocaust is. Let's not to repeat it. And-- I-- that's number one, you know. I also lecture another-- on poetry, as I'm a writer. I love to write poetry. And I have poetry-- [INAUDIBLE] poetry books for published. And I also-- my Holocaust, I said, is number one. I'm compiling my book on the Holocaust. I also-- I'm working on a children's book. It's going to be a book of love instead of hatred and violence. And I, in addition to that, let's see, I compose music. So that keeps me going, you know. [LAUGHS] Those are my main goals. And my goal is to-- to talk about the Holocaust and also to instill love for poetry in the parts of the people. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and I hope I will have a long life, you know, [LAUGHS] and a-- and a busy one. I am the type of person who cannot just retire. And I will never retire from the things that I mentioned to you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you feel you survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why do I feel I survived? Because I think, you know, I am a person with a deep faith. And I am a very spiritual person. And I believe I had to survive, because God had a purpose. I think that I got a second chance, a privilege. I have to cherish that. And I have to be the spokeswoman for the others who were silenced forever by the Nazis.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's why I think I survived. Because I always put the question-- how come? I had no control over my fate. I had these very strange things-- that I was always saved at the last minute, somehow pulled out from the grip of death. And I tell you something. I had to come to the point in Bergen-Belsen when I was actually dying. And I was thinking, well, why did that happen? I almost died. I was dying. That was for a purpose, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I think that in order to speak for the others that didn't make, I have to really understand and feel what it is like to die unjustly, just because you are a part of the Hebrew generation, what it means to die. And what in general, it means to die under such terrible conditions, being innocent. And I think that God wanted me to feel the ultimate thing, so I can really talk for the others. I really believe in that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much. I think we'll introduce your family now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Can you put this on the table. I don't want to wear my glasses when they're doing this.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's our anniversary coming on the 21st. And I have a love poem for my husband, but I know I can't read it here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait, don't start yet. Wait a minute. I would like you-- the one thing I would like you to do, OK--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in the presence of your family, since they're not going to-- would you recite the requiem?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We'll end with that-- if you'd like to. You don't have to.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That's fine. That's fine. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: I thought it would be appropriate to end it with a beautiful-- if that's the one that you've picked out.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's fine, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was thinking of my love poem for my husband. But that won't fit here. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, I think the requiem. [LAUGTHER] OK. Magda, would you introduce your family, now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Here's my daughter, Monica Riekoff, and then my husband, Eugene Herzberger, for 49 years. It's going to be our wedding anniversary on the 21st of November. So we are a team for a long time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is not present today?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my son, Henry Herzberger. And then my two grandchildren, are Mira Ma Herzberger and Nathan Herzberger. And my daughter-in-law, who is Susan Herzberger.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to recite-- wait, before we do that. Would either of you like to say something?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I'd like to say something. I-- I just really admire my mother for going out there and reminding people of the Holocaust and of the terrible experiences that people have had. And I really feel such deep sorrow for everyone who's been through it. And I hope that we never, ever forget the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I have to tell you. I have to make a comment that at the time when I started my public speaking and I was invited to talk about the Holocaust, I didn't know if I really wanted to do it. And I asked my husband for advice. And he encouraged me. He encouraged me through all these years. I have a wonderful husband. And he said, if you think you can do, you should do it. And so he really has a share in that he encouraged me. And through all those years when I was traveling all around, and-- and he had lots of understanding. And he put up with me neglecting certain things, [LAUGHS] the house, and cooking.

speaker: interviewer
text: So-- so would you like me to read? Well, I would like to read the requiem, because I also wrote the music for it, which was performed in the city of Dubuque, where we lived previously before we moved to Arizona. And also, it was performed in the Judah Temple in Cedar Rapids. And I would like you to listen, maybe to the music, after that. I know you cannot record this.

speaker: interviewee
text: 'Requiem.' My eyes shed drops of tears into the pools of years for those I loved and lost. And the like a hunting ghost, my spirit roams around, searching for their unknown burial ground. They were the victims of their faiths, sentenced to deaths without a sin for worshiping the only God they believed in. They died without protest, with no rebellion, together with the 6 million innocent Jews in the Nazi camps of Germany. Oh, Lord, please help me to keep alive their memory. They died in vain. I witnessed their agony and pain. I heard their cries. Oh, Lord, our God, open for them the gates of paradise. Let their souls rest in peace. Forgive mankind's atrocities.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: And thank you. I really admire what you are doing for the Holocaust. And I will remember this day forever. It was wonderful to meet you. And I wish you good luck for all the other interviews. I think we have a kinship in that. And I respect that. And I respect Mr. Spielberg for doing this. And I know that he is-- he founded this foundation, the Visual History Foundation, for the survivors of the Holocaust. And I wish him good luck and for you, too, for your project. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Who was this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my father whose name is Herman Moses, of my mother, whose name is Serena Moses. And in the middle is me, Magda Herzberger. At--

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in 1931. I was about five years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was taken in the city of Cluj in Romania.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get the picture?

words.text: how did you get the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is very unusual. All these pictures, these old family pictures that we are going to see now, besides this, were given to a Hungarian friend of my father, prior to our deportation, prior actually to-- to the Holocaust. And he was keeping it for us. And then when my mother came first, before I came home from the camps. And she retrieved them. And what happened, these were small pictures, you see. And what I did here in Arizona, I took them to a photographer, and they enlarged it. Because the picture is just about, maybe 4 by 6 or so. And I wanted to make a family [INAUDIBLE]. This is my memorial wall that you're going to see now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my uncle. And his name is Yeno, Hungarian spelling. But actually, it's Eugene. Eugene Moses. And as you can see, he has a very athletic body, because he was a great athlete. He was an Olympic athlete. He was the Romanian fencing champion. And I'm very proud of him. And I was in competitive fencing, because my uncle was a champion-- a fencing champion. And I had a little gold medal I won at age 13.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he died in the hall where my father died, in the-- he was a victim of the Holocaust. And I was very close to him. And I loved him. And I miss him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father, Herman Moss. And this picture was taken before my father was taken to the German concentration camps. And in this picture, he was 47 years old. But he always looked younger than his age.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was taken in Cluj, Romania.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this, again, my uncle, Eugene Moses. This picture was taken in 1930. So he was quite a bit younger at that time. And you can see that this is dedicated. This is his handwriting. And it's in Hungarian, which means, to my dear mother, from her loving son. And he has-- it was done in Arad, which is also in Romania. But it's not the city of Cluj. It's a different city. And it also contains the date.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is me, Magda Herzberger, at-- in 1926 age one. I was a pretty well-fed baby, I would say. [LAUGHING]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Who is this a picture of?

words.text: Who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is-- first of all, I point to myself. This is Magda Herzberger at age 3. And it was done in Turda, which is in Romania. And it is a summer resort. And some of my relatives lived there. And the others are cousins of my mother. And most of them died in the camps. The one in the middle was living in Israel. And she died about 4 years ago. And the-- the one girl at the extreme left was one of my cousins, who was-- who's also a survivor. She survived the camps. Whom you see beside the lady in the middle, and the one on the left, right, they were all killed in the German concentration camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first of all, I always talk about myself. Not that I'm egotistical, but-- I point out the girl at the extreme right in the first row. It's me. And I was about five years old. It's from the kindergarten. I am in a play, which I have the main role. I-- I wanted to be an actress. And-- and I was very happy, because I had the most beautiful Romanian national outfit. And this you can notice it. These are the national Romanian costumes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is again, me, Magda Herzberger. And I-- I loved dancing. Not only that I wanted to be an actress, but I wanted to be a dancer, too. So this is a little kindergarten dance. And I always was pushing for the main roles. And actually, I was-- I made a concession at that early age of mine. They said-- they told me that I had to be a boy. And I said, I don't want to be a boy. I want to be a girl. I'm a girl in the other picture. And they said, well, this time, in this dance, you have to be a boy. And if you don't want to be a boy, you're not going to be in the dance. I said, OK. I want to be in the dance. And I even put up a smile. I wanted to be accepting this gracefully.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is again, me, Magda Herzberger. That's the way I looked when I came back from the German concentration camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken in-- 19-- at the end of 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in Cluj Romania.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my wedding picture. Actually, it's Magda Herzberger, myself, and my husband, Eugene Herzberger. And this, you notice, we are dressed in black.

speaker: interviewer
text: But we are married for 49 years, so it worked out well for us. And you would question, why black? Because it was after the war. And we didn't have enough money. So these are-- we had second-hand clothing. But it worked. What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where?

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was in the city of Cluj Romania.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's this?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is a picture of my mother, Serena Moses [INAUDIBLE], because she remarried in Israel. And the picture was taken in 1990.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in Dubuque, Iowa, United States. And this is my son, Henry Herzberger, and my daughter-in-law, Susan Herzberger. And the picture was taken in about 19-- let's see-- 1988 or something like that. Yeah. I have more recent pictures, but then, this seems to be [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is my granddaughter, Mira Ma Herzberger, and my grandson, Nathan Herzberger. And the picture was taken last year, in 1994.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, here are my four books, published books. And two of them are exclusively poetry, as I'm a poet. But I also write prose. One is titled And it's poetry of a variety of subjects. Then It's a collection of love poems I dedicated for my husband for our 20th wedding anniversary. And there are the two books on the Holocaust. autobiographical. It's an autobiography, totally poetry form. But it has my life story and the story of the 6 million innocent Jews in this. And then has prose and narratives combined forming units. And I-- I put lots of myself. And this is like a memorial to all the victims of the Holocaust, including my family.
